title: Configuration Patterns
intro: Good configuration patterns keep your app flexible and secure, just like a car dashboard lets you adjust settings without rebuilding the engine. A developer hardcoded values that should be configurable. Find the bug that will cause problems in production.

teach.title: Config = A Car's Dashboard
teach.body: A car's dashboard lets you adjust things without rebuilding the engine -- temperature, radio station, mirror positions. **Configuration** in software works the same way. You should be able to change settings (database URL, API endpoints, feature flags) without rewriting code.

teach.title: Config = TV Remote Buttons
teach.body: A TV remote has buttons for volume, channel, and input -- you can change any of them without opening up the TV and rewiring it. Good configuration patterns work the same way: you should be able to swap your database, change an API key, or toggle a feature without touching a single line of code. Hardcoding values is like soldering the channel dial to channel 5 -- it works at your house but nobody else can use it.

teach.title: The #1 Config Rule: Never Hardcode Secrets
teach.body:
The most important rule: **never put values that change between environments directly in your code**. Instead, read them from environment variables:

- Database URLs
- API keys and secrets
- Service endpoints
- Feature flags
- Port numbers

teach.title: Bad vs Good Configuration
teach.annotation: The fallback pattern (|| 3000) lets you skip setting PORT in development while requiring it in production.

teach.body: Clean configuration patterns mean you can launch the same vibecoded app for different clients just by changing env vars. One codebase, multiple revenue streams.
Never hardcode values that differ between environments. Use process.env for everything configurable, with sensible defaults for development.

exercise.prompt: This app works fine locally but in production the API calls all fail. The developer used env vars for most config but missed one. Find the hardcoded value.
exercise.explanation: The API URL is hardcoded to 'http://localhost:4000/api'. This works on the developer's machine but fails in production because there is no localhost -- the API lives at a different URL on the server. The fix reads from an environment variable (API_URL) and falls back to localhost for development. This pattern -- env var with a default -- is the standard approach.
